content.full_text:
RAvI MEHTA
CHAI RMA N
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
October 10,
1996
Ms.
DeDe Lind
Commission for the Establishment
of Academic Content and
Performance Standards
1121
L Street,
Ste.
600
Sacramento,
CA 95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-96--271
Dear Ms.
Lind:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the
“Act”) •1
QUESTIONS
1.
Are members of the Commission for the Establishment of
Academic Content and Performance Standards
(the
“Commission”)
public officials subject to the conflict of interest and
disclosure provisions of the Act?
2.
Is the Commission required to adopt a conflict of
interest code?
CONCLUS IONS
1.
Yes.
Because the Commission possesses governmental
decisionmaking authority with respect to contracts, its members
are public officials subject to the conflict of interest and
disclosure provisions of the Act.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
FPPC regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
.g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


Lind,
DeDe
Our File No. A-96-271
Page
2
2.
Because the members are public officials under the Act
the Commission must adopt a conflict of interest code.
FACTS
The Commission was created by Stats.
1995,
ch.
975
(A.B.
265)
which specifies its responsibilities and which was amended by
Stats.
1996,
ch.
69
(S.B.
430)
The enabling legislation provided for a sunset date of
January 1,
1998.
However,
you indicated on the telephone that
legislation was passed extending the sunset date to June 30,
1998.
It is our understanding that this bill has not yet been acted upon
by the Governor.
S.B.
430 provided for an appropriation of $75,000 from the
General Fund to the Commission during the 1995-96 fiscal year.
However,
you indicated that the Commission did not receive those
funds because the legislation was signed very late,
June 21,
1996,
in the 1995-96 fiscal year.
You also indicated that the
Department of Finance in the 1996/97 budget is making $625,000
available to the Commission.
The Commission consists of 21 members,
including the
Superintendent of Public Instruction and appointees of the
Governor,
the Superintendent of Public Instruction,
and the
Legislature.
The members serve at the pleasure of the appointing
authority.
The legislation provides that the Commission shall develop
academically rigorous content and performance standards to be used
in public schools maintaining kindergarten and grades
1 to 12.
Those standards shall be measurable and objective, reflect the
knowledge and skills necessary for California’s work force to be
competitive in the global,
information-based economy of the 21st
Century,
be comparable in rigor to academic content and
performance standards used in the school systems of America’s
global economic competitors,
and provide the basis for assessments
for kindergarten and grades
1 through 12.
The Commission shall hold at least six public hearings
throughout the state to solicit public input regarding the
development of its content and performance standards.
On or before October 1,
1997,
the Commission shall have
developed and submitted the content and performance standards for
all core curriculum areas for all grade levels to the State Board
of Education for approval by the board.
No later than January 1,
1998 the State Board of Education shall adopt statewide content
and performance standards, pursuant to the recommendations of the
Commission in all core curriculum areas to serve as the basis for
assessing the academic achievement of individual pupils and of
schools,
school districts,
and the California education system.
The board may modify any proposed content or performance standards
prior to adoption and may adopt content and performance standards


Lind,
DeDe
Our File No. A-96-271
Page
3
in individual core curriculum areas as those standards are
submitted to the board by the Commission.
The Commission may appoint personnel and enter into contracts
and interagency agreements for services in support of the
Commission.
ANALYSIS
The Act prohibits a public official from making,
participating in making, or in any way attempting to influence a
governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to
know he or she has a financial interest.
(Section 87100.)
The conflict of interest provisions of the Act apply only to
“public officials.”
A public official is defined as every member,
officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local government
agency.
(Section 82048.)
For purposes of the Act,
the Commission
is a state agency.
Regulation 18700 (a) (1) provides that
“member”
includes, but is not limited to, salaried or unsalaried members of
boards or commissions with decisionmaking authority.
A board or
commission possesses decisionmaking authority whenever:
(A)
It may make a final governmental decision;
(B)
It may compel a governmental decision; or it may
prevent a governmental decision either by reason of an
exclusive power to initiate the decision or by reason of
a veto which may not be overridden; or
(C)
It makes substantive recommendations which are,
and
over an extended period of time have been, regularly
approved without significant amendment or modification
by another public official or governmental agency.
If an agency meets any of the tests of Regulation
18700 (a) (1),
(B)
or
(C), it possesses decision making authority,
and its board members are deemed public officials subject to the
conflict-of-interest requirements of the Act.
Regulation 18700(b) provides that a public official
“makes a
governmental decision,” when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position:
(1)
Votes on a matter;
(2)
Appoints a person:
(3)
Obligates or commits his or her agency to any
course of action:
(4)
Enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
his or her agency;... (Emphasis added.)


Lind,
DeDe
Our File No. A-96-271
Page
4
Since the Commission has the authority to enter into
contracts,
members of the Commission are considered public
officials under the Act because they are making governmental
decisions and therefore are subject to the disclosure and
disqualification provisions of the Act.
Therefore,
Section 87300
requires the agency to adopt and promulgate a conflict of interest
code.
Enclosed is a packet of information concerning adoption of a
code.
The code should include the members of the Commission as
well as any other employees of the Commission which make or
participate in the making of governmental decisions,
as designated
employees who are obligated to file statements of economic
interests and are subject to the Act’s disqualification
provisions
2
If you have additional questions, please contact
me at
(916)
322-5662.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Coisel
c/J
By:
ayne P.
Imberi
Political Reform Consultant
enc.
2
Any members of the Commission who also hold offices which are
specified in Section 87200,
such as the Superintendent of Public
Instruction,
are exempt from filing statements of economic
interest pursuant to the Commission’s conflict of interest code.
